statement.normalized: We call $m$ practical if every integer $1\leq n<m$ is the sum of distinct divisors of $m$. If $m$ is practical then let $h(m)$ be such that $h(m)$ many divisors always suffice. Are there infinitely many practical $m$ such that\[h(m) < (\log\log m)^{O(1)}?\]Is it true that $h(n!)<n^{o(1)}$? Or perhaps even $h(n!)<(\log n)^{O(1)}$?
statement.original:
We call $m$ practical if every integer $1\leq n<m$ is the sum of distinct divisors of $m$. If $m$ is practical then let $h(m)$ be such that $h(m)$ many divisors always suffice.

Are there infinitely many practical $m$ such that\[h(m) < (\log\log m)^{O(1)}?\]Is it true that $h(n!)<n^{o(1)}$? Or perhaps even $h(n!)<(\log n)^{O(1)}$?
